message.content:
Below is a **source catalog** of the most authoritative published standards, guidance, and registries you would mine to enumerate launch-readiness tests for a web application. I’ve grouped them by the domains you named and focused on **canonical sources** rather than vendor blog posts.

| Source | Organization | Categories of tests / checks it defines | Canonical URL |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Web Security Testing Guide (WSTG)** | Open Web Application Security Project (OWASP) | Web application **security testing** guidance; covers information gathering, configuration/deployment, identity/authentication, authorization, session management, input validation, error handling, client-side testing, API testing, and business logic testing. | `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/` |
| **Application Security Verification Standard (ASVS)** | OWASP | A **security verification test taxonomy** with control levels and requirements for authentication, session management, access control, input validation, cryptography, error handling, malicious code, business logic, and API/web service security. | `https://owasp.org/www-project-application-security-verification-standard/` |
| **OWASP API Security Top 10** | OWASP | **API security** risk categories to test for, including broken object-level authorization, broken authentication, broken function-level authorization, unrestricted resource consumption, injection, and security misconfiguration. | `https://owasp.org/www-project-api-security/` |
| **OWASP API Security Testing Guide** | OWASP | Practical **API security testing** procedures and test ideas for authentication, authorization, rate limiting, input validation, schema validation, and object-level access control. | `https://owasp.org/www-project-api-security-testing-guide/` |
| **OWASP Top 10** | OWASP | High-level **web application security** risk categories commonly used to structure security test coverage, including access control, cryptographic failures, injection, insecure design, security misconfiguration, vulnerable components, and identification/authentication failures. | `https://owasp.org/www-project-top-ten/` |
| **NIST SP 800-115, Technical Guide to Information Security Testing and Assessment** | National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) | Authoritative **security testing** methods and assessment categories such as review, scanning, vulnerability testing, penetration testing, and security assessment planning. | `https://csrc.nist.gov/publications/detail/sp/800-115/final` |
| **WAI Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.2** | W3C Web Accessibility Initiative (WAI) | **Accessibility** conformance criteria for perceivable, operable, understandable, and robust content; these become testable checks for text alternatives, contrast, keyboard access, focus order, labels, error identification, and more. | `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/` |
| **WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices Guide (APG)** | W3C WAI | **Accessibility interaction tests** for common widgets and patterns, especially keyboard behavior, focus management, and accessible name/role/state expectations. | `https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/` |
| **Accessibility Conformance Testing (ACT) Rules Format** | W3C | A standard way to express **accessibility test rules** and expected outcomes in machine-usable form. | `https://www.w3.org/WAI/standards-guidelines/act/rules-format/` |
| **Chrome Lighthouse** | Google | Automated checks for **performance**, **accessibility**, **SEO**, and **best practices**; especially useful for launch readiness triage. | `https://developer.chrome.com/docs/lighthouse/overview/` |
| **Core Web Vitals** | Google | User-centric **performance** metrics and thresholds: Largest Contentful Paint (LCP), Interaction to Next Paint (INP), and Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS). | `https://web.dev/vitals/` |
| **Web.dev Performance guidance** | Google | Broader **web performance** testing and optimization guidance, including rendering, loading, responsiveness, and measurement methods. | `https://web.dev/learn/performance/` |
| **Google Search Central documentation** | Google | **SEO** and structured-data guidance: crawlability, indexing, canonicalization, metadata, sitemaps, robots directives, and search appearance. | `https://developers.google.com/search/docs` |
| **Schema.org** | Schema.org community initiative | The core vocabulary for **structured data** types and properties used in SEO and rich-result eligibility tests. | `https://schema.org/` |
| **Rich Results Test** | Google | Validation of pages for **structured-data / rich-result** eligibility and markup errors. | `https://search.google.com/test/rich-results` |
| **Search Console documentation** | Google | **SEO diagnostics** and index coverage concepts used to test whether pages are discoverable and indexable. | `https://support.google.com/webmasters/` |
| **Web Platform Tests (WPT)** | W3C / WHATWG / browser vendors | Cross-browser **functional / interoperability** test suite covering HTML, DOM, CSS, Web APIs, accessibility-adjacent behavior, and many browser features. | `https://web-platform-tests.org/` |
| **Test262** | Ecma International | Conformance tests for **JavaScript** behavior, useful for functional correctness of client-side code. | `https://github.com/tc39/test262` |
| **WHATWG HTML Standard** | WHATWG | The normative definition of **HTML behavior**; it is not a test catalog itself, but it is the authoritative spec from which HTML feature tests are derived. | `https://html.spec.whatwg.org/` |
| **MDN Web Docs: HTTP headers** | Mozilla | Reference for **HTTP header** behavior and semantics; useful when converting header requirements into test cases, but it is a documentation source rather than a standard. | `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers` |
| **HTTP Semantics** | Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) | Canonical semantics for **HTTP behaviors** that drive testable header and response-handling expectations. | `https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110` |
| **HTTP Security Response Headers** | Mozilla Observatory | A widely used checklist for testing **security-related HTTP headers** such as CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, and Permissions-Policy. | `https://observatory.mozilla.org/` |

A few notes on how to use this set:

- For **web security** and **API security**, OWASP’s **WSTG**, **ASVS**, and **API Security Testing Guide** are the most directly useful catalogs for enumerating actual tests.[1][2]
- For **accessibility**, the most authoritative testable source is **WCAG 2.2**, with **WAI-ARIA APG** for interactive widget behavior and **ACT Rules** for formalized test rules.[3][4][5]
- For **performance/Core Web Vitals**, use **Core Web Vitals** plus Lighthouse and broader web.dev performance guidance.[6][7][8]
- For **SEO/structured data**, pair **Google Search Central** with **Schema.org** and the **Rich Results Test**.[9][10]
- For **functional/E2E** and broader browser behavior, **Web Platform Tests** is the main interoperability catalog, while **Test262** covers JavaScript conformance.
- For **HTTP/TLS headers**, the authoritative basis is the relevant IETF RFCs for HTTP semantics and TLS; for security-header testing specifically, Mozilla Observatory is a practical checklist source.

If you want, I can turn this into a **master audit checklist** with one line per test family, or split it into **must-have vs nice-to-have** sources for launch readiness.
